questions.prompt: What are the four operating principles of Tesla's manufacturing approach?

choices.text: First principles (not industry convention), factory-as-product, vertical integration where it controls outcomes, software-defined operations. Apply these and you end up with different cars and different factories — the gigacasting, structural battery, vertical campus model emerges from the combination.

questions.explanation: Tesla's approach is the combination of these four. Individual pieces aren't unique (cell-to-pack came from BYD; gigacasting existed in aerospace) — what's distinctive is committing to all four simultaneously while launching a new product line.

questions.prompt: A founder reads about Tesla's gigacasting and wants to apply 'Tesla-style first-principles manufacturing' to a $50/unit consumer good. Right move?

choices.text: Probably not. Tesla's approach fits capital-intensive, physics-bounded products with long cycle times and large unit prices. For a $50 consumer good, the capex of vertical integration + custom factories vs. existing supplier networks usually doesn't pencil out. Apply principles selectively (question conventions, instrument operations) rather than copying the full Tesla playbook.

questions.explanation: Tesla's full approach requires huge capital and a long horizon. The principles transfer; the literal playbook doesn't. Match the strategy to your product economics; selectively borrow what fits.
